straight_beat.name: Bobby Lee Chamberlain
straight_beat.column:
The Royals’ 2-0 win over the White Sox yesterday wasn’t the kind of game that makes you slap your forehead and yell, “That’s the Midwest box!” It was the kind of game that makes you lean back, sip your coffee, and mutter, “That’s the Midwest box.” You know the one. The kind of game where the ball doesn’t travel far, the infielders don’t make mistakes, and the pitchers—well, the pitchers are just doing their job.  

Witt Jr. singled home two, Lugo pitched into the seventh, and Kansas City beat the Twins 4

optimist.name: Dottie Verlander (no relation)
optimist.column:
The Royals walked away from Sunday’s game with a 2-0 win over the White Sox, and if you’re looking for a reason to believe in this team, look no further than Bobby Witt Jr. The kid’s been a steady flame in the middle of the lineup, and I’m not just saying that because I’ve been grilling him since he was in Double-A. No, this is about the way he’s carried himself, the way he’s made every at-bat feel like a slow-burn brisket—patient, deliberate, and finally done right.  

Witt’s not just hitting; he’s making hitters think. The White Sox’ pitching staff

pessimist.name: Cletus Marburger
pessimist.column:
The Royals lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that feels familiar, like a punchline to a joke we’ve heard 30 times. Yesterday’s 2-0 defeat to the White Sox wasn’t just another loss—it was a mirror held up to the franchise’s soul. The record now sits at 7-8, two games behind the division lead, and the AL Central looks like a playoff race that’s been decided before the first pitch. You don’t need a crystal ball to see this: the Royals are stuck in a loop, a cycle of hope and disappointment that’s played out for decades.  

The front office has always been the architect of this loop. David Glass, the man who once promised a championship in 1985, is still the ghost in the boardroom, shaping decisions with a hand that’s more heavy than helpful. You get one or two good players, they get traded. You get a ring, they fire-sale the roster. It’s not about strategy—it’s about scarcity. The Royals don’t build teams; they borrow them. Last season’s “contender” was a collection of rentals, players acquired for a cup of coffee and a promise of tomorrow. Tomorrow never came.  

Look at the roster today. There’s talent, sure—some of it real, some of it borrowed from other teams’ trash piles. But depth? Sustainability? Those words don’t appear in the front office’s vocabulary. They’re too busy chasing the next hot prospect, the next “fluke” season, the next chance to sell a ticket to a game that’s more about the stadium’s lights than the game itself. The 2023 Royals are a team that plays well enough to win, but never enough to stay. They’re the kind of team that makes
